metadata.career_relevance: Razorpay's payment gRPC service processes 1M+ transactions daily. A bug in the streaming RPC that sends payment confirmations crashed the service for 12 minutes — ₹18 crore in missed transactions. The bug was in an edge case of the stream observer that was never tested. After this topic, you can write comprehensive gRPC tests using InProcessServer (no network, fast, deterministic) that would have caught this bug in CI.

hook.what_you_will_build: InProcess gRPC test harness for Razorpay payment service — unary and streaming.

visual_aid.description: gRPC InProcess test architecture. Left: Test class with @BeforeEach setup. Center: InProcessServer (no network, in JVM memory) containing the real PaymentServiceImpl. Right: Test stub connected via InProcessChannel (same JVM, no TCP). Arrows: test → stub (blocking/async) → InProcessChannel → InProcessServer → PaymentServiceImpl → response back. Below: TestStreamObserver diagram showing onNext()/onCompleted()/onError() callbacks and CountDownLatch for async synchronization.
visual_aid.alt_text: InProcessServer: gRPC tests with no real network — server and client in same JVM

worked_example.scenario: Test suite for Razorpay PaymentService — unary createPayment and streaming streamConfirmations.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. InProcessServerBuilder setup in @BeforeEach
2. Test unary createPayment: PaymentRequest → PaymentResponse
3. Test streaming: stream confirmations → verify all statuses received
4. Test error path: amount = 0 → StatusRuntimeException INVALID_ARGUMENT
5. @SpringBootTest or plain JUnit

step_by_step_solution.details: directExecutor(): runs gRPC in the same thread as the test (no thread pool). Tests become deterministic — no race conditions between test thread and gRPC threads. Never use directExecutor() in production (blocks the event loop). Tests only.

worked_example.expected_output:
✓ createPayment_returns_paymentId (15ms)
✓ createPayment_zeroAmount_throwsInvalidArgument (8ms)
✓ streamConfirmations_sends3Updates (22ms)
✓ streamConfirmations_largeAmount_completesNormally (18ms) ← would have caught the bug!
things_to_notice: No real network: InProcessServer uses a named pipe within the JVM. Tests run in ~10-50ms vs 100-500ms for real network. 10x faster CI.
Real implementation: InProcess tests run your REAL service code (not mocks). This is integration testing at the gRPC level — the proto serialization/deserialization is also tested.
CountDownLatch for streaming: asynchronous streams need synchronization. onCompleted() or onError() decrements the latch. Test waits up to 5 seconds (never hangs CI forever).
Test isolation: each test gets a uniquely named server (InProcessServerBuilder.generateName()). Tests don't share server state and can run in parallel.

industry_examples.use_case: Every PaymentService RPC has an InProcess test. Streaming RPCs: TestStreamObserver captures all messages, asserting correct status sequence (INITIATED→PROCESSING→CONFIRMED). Edge cases: amount > ₹10L, currency mismatch, gateway timeout — all tested with InProcess. CI: 200 gRPC tests run in 8 seconds total (InProcess). Would take 3+ minutes with real network stubs.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you test gRPC services?' → InProcessServerBuilder: starts gRPC server in same JVM (no network). Add real service implementation, connect InProcessChannelBuilder. Test unary: blocking stub, assert response fields. Test streaming: TestStreamObserver with CountDownLatch. Test error: assertThrows(StatusRuntimeException.class, ...) then check getStatus().getCode(). Test interceptors: add interceptor to InProcessServer, test it's applied.
applications.real_world_workflow: gRPC test workflow: (1) generate a unique server name, (2) start InProcessServer with real service impl, (3) connect InProcessChannel, (4) create stubs (blocking for unary, async for streaming), (5) TestStreamObserver for streaming, (6) @AfterEach shutdown server and channel, (7) use directExecutor for deterministic single-threaded tests.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Asserting streaming results immediately without waiting for stream to complete. Observer.getValues() is empty because the async stream hasn't delivered messages yet.
common_gaps.remediation: Always call observer.awaitCompletion() before asserting. CountDownLatch with 5-second timeout: fails CI rather than hanging indefinitely. Alternative: use directExecutor() — synchronous execution makes streaming tests simpler.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: assertEquals(3, observer.getValues().size()) fails with 0 elements — stream not awaited before assertion

key_takeaways: InProcessServerBuilder: gRPC server in same JVM (no network, fast, deterministic)
InProcessChannelBuilder.forName(same name): connects to in-process server
directExecutor(): single-threaded, no race conditions — tests only
BlockingStub for unary: synchronous test code
CountDownLatch: wait for stream completion before asserting
awaitCompletion(5 seconds): never hang CI indefinitely
getStatus().getCode(): verify specific gRPC status code

self_check_questions.question: Why use InProcessServer instead of mocking the service interface for gRPC tests?
self_check_questions.answer: Mocking the service: you test the business logic in isolation but skip gRPC serialization, proto validation, and the gRPC framework itself. If a proto field is wrong type or a required field is missing — mock tests pass but real gRPC call would fail. InProcessServer: tests run the REAL service implementation through the REAL gRPC framework. Proto messages are serialized/deserialized. Request validation happens. Interceptors run. The full gRPC stack is exercised. Only the network is replaced (InProcess pipe vs TCP). Trade-off: InProcess tests are slower than pure mocks (~15ms vs ~1ms) but faster than real network (~15ms vs ~150ms). For gRPC: InProcess is the sweet spot — catches real proto/serialization bugs without network overhead. Pure mocks: use for unit testing pure business logic that doesn't depend on gRPC.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 28 complete! You've mastered gRPC: protobuf, Spring Boot gRPC, streaming, security, and testing. Combined with GraphQL (M27), you now have the full modern API toolkit.
